message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In Japan, as a countermeasure against the COVID-19 outbreak, voluntary restrictions against going out (VRG) have been applied.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Object</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We examined mobility information provided by Apple Inc. to a susceptible–infected–recovery model.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>When applying a polynomial function to daily Apple data with the SIR model, we presumed the function up to a cubic term as in our earlier study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Estimation results demonstrated R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    as 1.507 and its 95% confidence interval was [1.502, 1.509].. The estimated coefficients of Apple data was 1.748 and its 95% confidence interval was [1.731, 1.788].
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion and Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Results show that mobility data from Apple Inc. can explain the entire course of the outbreak in COVID-19 in Japan. Therefore, monitoring Apple data might be sufficient to adjust control measures to maintain an effective reproduction number of less than one.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Mobility data can explain the entire COVID-19 outbreak course in Japan

reference.unstructured: Kamikubo Y . Takahashi A . Paradoxical dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 by herd immunity and antibody-dependent enhancement. https://www.cambridge.org/engage/coe/article-details/5ead2b518d7bf7001951c5a5 [accessed on June 5, 2020]
